steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Text 1 says Asimov's characters are flat. The question asks how the author of Text 2 would respond to that claim. Text 2 argues that Asimov never intended to develop characters — his flat characterization serves a deliberate thematic purpose (conveying the insignificance of individuals across vast historical timescales).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'flatness is a feature of Robots and Empire and I, Robot but not Foundation': Text 2 actually uses Foundation as its main example of flat characterization. This gets the facts backwards. ✗
  • Choice B — 'characterizations would be more believable if the writing discussed fewer ideas': Text 2 never suggests Asimov should have discussed fewer ideas — it defends his approach as intentional. ✗
  • Choice C — 'characterizations would be richer with a deeper understanding of history': Text 2 says flat characterization is central to his thematic aims around history — it doesn't suggest he lacked historical understanding. ✗
  • Choice D — 'flatness should not necessarily be considered a flaw': Text 2 explicitly argues Asimov's lack of characterization was deliberate and 'central to his thematic aims,' so calling it a flaw is wrong. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
